participants.extracted: Pregnant adult women with HIV infection living in malaria-endemic areas in Kenya, Mozambique, and Tanzania. Women of all gravidities attending antenatal care for the first time were eligible if they had not received IPTp during the current pregnancy, had a gestational age of 28 weeks or less, were permanent residents in the study area, had a positive HIV test, and had no history of allergy to sulfa drugs or mefloquine, severe renal/hepatic/psychiatric/neurological disease, or recent mefloquine/halofantrine use. A total of 1,071 women were randomized (537 control, 534 mefloquine).

interventions.extracted: Monthly intermittent preventive treatment in pregnancy (IPTp) with mefloquine (MQ) given in addition to daily cotrimoxazole prophylaxis (CTXp; trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole 800/160 mg tablet) and long-lasting insecticide-treated nets. Women in the intervention arm received three supervised monthly doses of MQ at 15 mg/kg (maximum 1,500 mg of MQ base per dose), starting at gestational age at least 13 weeks, with repeat dosing if vomiting occurred shortly after administration. The comparator arm received an identical placebo in place of MQ, while both groups continued CTXp and received a LLITN at enrolment.

outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure the following outcomes: primary outcome—prevalence of peripheral maternal malaria infection at delivery, defined as the presence of asexual P. falciparum parasites of any density detected by blood smear and/or PCR, assessed at delivery; secondary outcomes—placental infection at delivery, defined by parasites with or without pigment detected by placental histology, impression smear, or PCR, assessed at delivery; maternal anemia at delivery, defined as hemoglobin <11 g/dl, and severe maternal anemia, defined as hemoglobin <7 g/dl, assessed at delivery; fetal anemia, defined as cord blood hemoglobin <12.5 g/dl, assessed at delivery; maternal parasitemia one month after delivery, assessed at 1 month postpartum; fetal parasitemia one month after delivery, assessed at 1 month postpartum; maternal CD4+ T-cell count at delivery, categorized as ≤350 or >350 cells/µl, assessed at delivery; maternal viral load at delivery, analyzed on the logarithmic scale, assessed at delivery; incidence of clinical malaria episodes during follow-up, defined by parasitemia plus fever and/or other malaria symptoms, assessed throughout pregnancy follow-up; incidence of all-cause outpatient attendance during follow-up, assessed throughout pregnancy follow-up; incidence of all-cause hospital admissions during follow-up, assessed throughout pregnancy follow-up; incidence of non-obstetric hospital admissions during follow-up, assessed throughout pregnancy follow-up; immediate tolerability of study drug, measured by observed vomiting within one hour after administration, assessed after each IPTp dose; adverse events and serious adverse events, including miscarriage, stillbirth, cutaneous and neuropsychiatric events, and other SAEs, assessed during pregnancy and follow-up; maternal and neonatal death, assessed during pregnancy and the postpartum period; pregnancy outcomes including miscarriage, stillbirth, premature birth, and congenital malformations, assessed at delivery; mother-to-child transmission of HIV, measured by infant HIV DNA PCR at 6 weeks of age, assessed at 6 weeks postpartum; infant survival and general morbidity, assessed up to 2 months after birth; adherence to cotrimoxazole prophylaxis and adherence to LLITN use, assessed at monthly antenatal visits and unscheduled visits; and adherence to PMTCT/ART regimens, assessed during pregnancy and at delivery.
